regions.text: SPECIAL TREAT

regions.label: text
regions.text: The Paradise Boys will give a big dance frolic and Carnival. Thursday evening Die. 1, at the Paradise Dance place, the sweetest hall in St. Louis. Couvents will be given free to every one. Come and dance to the music of the ultra-famous jazz band of Mr. Chas. Creath.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: RUTH SHELTON'S DRAMA

regions.label: text
regions.text:
On Opening Night Of Y. W. C. A.
Bazaar, Thursday, Dec. 1.
The Y. W. C. A. Bazaar Committee
have been fortunate in securing the
night of the Bazaar, at which time
the dramatist will present her comedy
"All love and no money." This play is
one of the four popular plays written
by Mrs. Shelton for her bhauqanuq
last July. Hundreds of people throng
ed to the grounds each night to see
them. The comedy is sparkling with
wilt and humor and will pack the house.
Hutchinson and Millon have the lead
ing role with a well-balanced cast.

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: ULYSSES: THE GREAT AMERICA'S NOTED MYSTIFIER HERE

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Will Fill Engagement at Several St. Louis Churches

U. S. J. of Atlanta, Georgia, better known as "Ulysses the Great," will appear for the first time in St. Louis, Monday night, Nov. 25, at Jones Chapel, A. M. E. Z. Church, 4216 W. Lexington Avenue, Rev. A. L. Wilson, pastor.

His clean, mystifying, entertaining wonder plays exhibited at East St. Louis last Monday night under the nipples of Zion A. M. E. Church, Rev. E. J. Dodson, pastor, gave artistic satisfaction to the audience and by a real response was given a dramatic en-agement, Thursday night Dec. 1.

Ulysses come, nightly recommend-ely some of the most prominent high- ranking officials and leading daily parish throughout Americas as well as large presidents and ministers of the finest caliber. That Ulysses is a wonderful success at his edifying profession is worthy proven by the number of wise worthy letters from such dis- signified men as the late Booker T. Washington, the late M. E. Z. Church, the late Gaponon Theodoric Seminary, Atlanta, Ga.; Harry Andrews, presiding; preschool Clark University, Atlanta, Ga.; Dr. Ernest Hale, pastor, Baptist Church Atlanta, Ga.; B. D. Dayls, editor Atlanta Independent, and hundreds of others.

His engagements next week are as follows: Monday, Nov. 25, Jones Chapel, A. M. E. Z. Church, Rev. W. Nelson, pastor; Tuesday night, Nov. 29, Provident Baptist Church, Rev. E. C. Cole, pastor; Thursday night, Dec. 1, Provident Baptist Church, Rev. E. J. Dodson, pastor.

One of his real outstanding features demonstrated, at each performance will be the Duplication of Spirit Manifestation. You will laugh marvel and go away inspired.

COLORED AND WHITE DANCERS
IN CHAMPIONSHIP CONTENT

regions.label: text
regions.text:
A dancing Center between the best dancers of St. Louis will be given at Pythian Hall on Thursday night, December 1. under the auspices of the Supreme Twelve Club. An unstinted feature of the affair is the fact that the Colored dancers will be put to the test of competing with white artists in that line, in answer to their谆y, "Are We The Best Dancers?" As a result the following whites: Joe Beckert, Al Strain and Allison McCourt. Jude Lempke, Michel McNey and Henry Burgert. Cash prices will range from 10 to 50. Silly Costello's Mamhistory Saturdays will furnish the mash. The allusion will be 10 and there will be dancing from 5 to 11.

SEVEN CHURCH BAPTIST UNION

Services as usual. President read 10th charter of Indiana 12th verse, "My Lord Lay Us Lovely, O Lord, prayed by Mary Martin." The allusion provided a stir and interest in the fun of our invitation. We are eager to see you.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: COOK AVE. S. D. A. CHURCH

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Cor. Cook and Sarah

regions.label: text
regions.text:
We are now conducting three weekly services each week. Sunday, Wednesday and Friday nights. An intense interesting lecture on some popular subject each evening.

Friday night, Nov. 25, "Two men want to heaven and came back to earth what did they say about the play?" Friday night, Nov. 27, "Did Christ preach hell: How many were converted?" Wednesday night, Nov. 30, "Where do people go when they die? to Hell, Purgatory, or Paradise?" Friday night, Dec. 4, "A time coming when men will give a fortune to hear a sermon." These lectures have helped many to see the Bible in a new light. Thinking people today are every bit of a thinking for an intelligent understanding of the Bible. God blesses the speaker with a remarkable understanding of the most difficult portion of the Holy scriptures. The public is invited. Lula B. Bell, missionary secretary.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: QUIETLY MARRIED

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Jas. H. Tanter and Mrs. Blanche
Ferguson McCall were quietly married
at the marriage of Beren Prespiterian
Church last Tuesday evening. Nov. 22
Only a few members of the family
were present. Dr. S. W. Parr, the pass-
tor, performed the ceremony.
Mrs. Tanter is a sister-in-law of
Mrs. Walphin Moore, and Mrs. Tanter
is a clerk in the local postoffice.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: CAR OF THANKS

regions.label: text
regions.text:
August 9. Franklin, beloved son of
Mr. and Mrs Thomas A. Franklin
entered into reef Wednesday. November
9 at 11:15 a.m.

We are thankful to Father Lymon
for his kindness in the sad hour of
our bereavement, also Rev. Stevens for
his kindness. We extend our appreciation
to our many friends for their
kindness and help in designating
the kindnesses for their accomplish-
ment and Mrs Russell understake. All
kindnesses are highly appreciated.
Sadly missed by sisters, brothers,
host of relatives and friends.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: IN MEMORIAM

regions.label: text
regions.text:
In, memorial of our beloved son
Adolph, who departed this life, just
one year ago today.

Though he be dead, yet does he rest in
the memory of our lives, so near and
so dear.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
In loving memory of our dear father,
Eugene Wellington, who departed this life
November 12, 1949; our brother,
Daniel Wellington; our sister,
Life November 21, 1943; and Willie
Wellington, who departed this life
November 1, 1949.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: IN MEMORIAM

regions.label: text
regions.text:
In having memory of my only son,
Columbus Walker, who departed this
life so suddenly three years ago, November
26, 1918.

Sad and suddenly was the call.
For my son, J. Iysegur.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
A shadow fell upon my life
When the death the reaper came,
And took my only son away
And left me all alone.

There are griefs that cannot find
comfort,
And wounds that cannot be healed.
For mother you did your best
May God grant you everlast rest.

Gracious one from us has gone.
A voice I loved is stillted.
A place is vacant in one home
Which never can be filled.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Sudly missed by mother, wife and cousin.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: 500 RENTERS

regions.label: text
regions.text:
During the last 10 years some 500 families of people have built or bought their homes in South Kindle Park near Ferguson, Missouri, and are now living in them. They have had more to eat, more fresh vegetables, more poultry, more eggs, more fresh meat, more employment, better sleep and better health with fewer deaths than any other families of the same station. They have been taken to Kindle, empty handed. Some of them have their places fully paid for, others have them partly paid for, and others are just starting. The money they have saved and put into these homes will vary from $200 to $900/each. Perhaps the average amount saved by each of these 500 families will be $600. 500 times $800 is $300,000— thus the owners actually saved $300,000 during the time they have been in Kindle. What have the owners spent their rent paid their rent 12 times each year, and among the 500, they have 600,000 little pieces of paper—rent receipts, worth nothing to them. They probably cost them an average $10,000 each; 60,000 times $10.00 is $300,000— thus the renters have spent and lost $300,000 while the owners saved $300,000. Choose ye this day when ye will serve—yourself or a landlord.

The Olive Street Terrace Realty Company, a strong reliable company worth $4,000,000 in visible assets, has sold 300 lots in this most prosperous subdivision, and is offering 300 to 400 opportunity in return. The opportunity is open to You and we can come to it. At the low price we are offering these lots, we cannot afford to bring you out—you must come out yourself. Take any car to Wollston, then take a northbound, Kirkwood-Ferguson car to Winton Station at South Kindle Park.

Cut this out and put $10,000 with it just now and get a credit for $20,000 any purchase you may make.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: DE SOTO, MO.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Rev. R. Clinton has returned from a three-week's vacation spent with his father at Columbus, Ky. He also spent two Sundays in Charleston, Mo. Rev. Clinton returned much improved in health and ready for another year's work. Remember the rally Nov. 27, to forget the meetings of the Parent Assembly, the Parental Advisory Board and Erickierk Alexander has recently purchased a home on the corner of Jorheim and Blow. **** Mark Jenkins is able to be out again with the aid of crutches. **** The churches are busy preparing for Thanksgiving. Dinner served all afternoon and evening in the basement of William's Chapel, Turkey and all its "fixings" will be Mrs. Kate Smith and Sam Smith. In place where she went to attend the wedding of Miss Irene Bisch who became the bride of Pearl Gray. **** The funeral of Mrs. Jenelle Mitchell was held at Williams' Chapel on Monday evening, at 2:30, conducted by Rev. Clinton, assisted by Rev. Higginbotham and DeBoe. Aunt Jude had been a deBoe. A Church for thirty five years. Being at the church and the possession of a sunny and cheerful disposition, she was loved by her fat disposition, she was loved by her white friends which covered the casket and their presence. A number of friends were also present. The family of the entire community is extended to Mr. Mitchell and family. **** Mrs. Alice Ransom died at the home of her daughter. Maggie Johnson. Valles Mines on the evening of Nov. 21, Mrs. Ransom had been ill several months, suffering from cancer. She had been a resident of Detroit for several years, a daughter of Mrs. Ed. Sibley. She was made a William's Chapel. The members extend sympathy to the children in this their hope of bereavement. Funeral services and burial Wednesday afternoon at Valles Mines.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: A. Williams

regions.label: text
regions.text: The covenant meeting at the First Baptist Church was discontinued Sunday night and instead, Rev. J. L. Fits of Rockford, IL, preached a powerful sermon, text from St. John, 3rd chapter and 5th verse, *Mr. Vivian Williams and Miss Lizzie Rolls were told to attend a church meeting, leader of Thursday night prepermeeting, asks that all members who can attend these meetings, Mr. Cohler has been a faithful leader of this meeting for the past seven years.* **Mr. Lawrence Henry and Mr. William Bailey have bought an automobile.* **Mr. William Phillips of South Chauvin is on the sick list. Mr. Wm. William Bailey is in her bed.* **Mr. Chara Davis has returned home after being in the hospital for a week. She is getting along nicely.**

regions.text: CRYSTAL CITY, MO.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By Mrs. S. H. Casey

regions.label: text
regions.text: Rev P. Chandler presides a wonderful aeration Sunday night. The members and friends of this community are trying to do everything they can for his success. Mr. and Mrs. Lankford Gray and Mrs. Henry Smith attended the funeral of Mrs. Elizabeth Mitchell at Desoto. **Mrs. Chlara Price of St. Louis is visiting her sister and husband. Mr. and Mrs. Johnson Solemon **. The comedy, "Twilight on Muskating Farm," wrote and dramatized by H. A. Cockrell, was a decided success. M. Pilgrim church was crowded to its capacity. There was an uprue of laughter from stary children. Muskating medleys were good. Cast of chaperones were Bills, L. Cassey H. Cockrell, Abby Mealie Smith, Benlah Smith; Mabel Bingham, Rolly Johnson. We extend to present to the public in the near future "All for the love of fair brown face" written by H. A. Cockrell, Mrs. S. V. Bean; music director. **Mrs. Vijah Burrows was the guest of Miss Edith Nelson of Herculaneum.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: EAST ST. LOUIS Y. W. C. A.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Miss Chambers is conducting quite a splendid Gym 'Class on Mondays' from 6:30 to 8:40 a.m. All who were present on Monday enjoyed it immensely.
Mrs. Cordella Winn, of the National Board New York, was a guest of the YMCA from Monday Noon to Tuesday, evening during which time, she hold several conferences and gare a great deal of helpful advice and counsel.
The Japanese Party of the Mother's Club on this Friday night promises to be quite a novel affair.
"The Feast of The Red Corn" an Indian play, will be presented at the Y. M. C. A. by the Y. W. C. A. girls on Wednesday. November 30th "Madens All Forfern" a novel play will be given on the 15th; "The Rainmakers" a play full of fun, heightened by being given on the 16th. This is a monster benefit for the poor of the city.
The various clubs and organizations are making things hum around the Center these days.

regions.label: text
regions.text: The usual services were held at the Virginia Street Baptist Church, Sunday, with Rev. G. S. Chinn in the pulpit. *P. Reynolds M. S. Bryant, P. E. held the first Quarterly Meeting of the Conference year for Rev. Wm. Jackson, pastor of St. James A. M. E. Church, the occasion was a great day for St. James, Collection $34.00. Harrush for St. James and her new preacher, Rev. Riley Jackson, he power-sown sermons and in the afternoon Jackson, the pastor preacher, a masterly sermon on "Watchfulness." The Rev. was at his best and preached an instructive and inspiring swamman from the text: "Watch and Pray, lest ye enter into temptation." His sun-divisions were: "Wach your words," Your actions, Your Thoughts, Your Character, Your Heart. The Rev. gave all of his hearers something to think about. regions.text: Mrs Marie Kennedy of Chester, IL. is visiting Mrs. Merrill Wyllie *M. Mr. Grant Matthews, our mail carrier* met with a painful accident Monday evening, while coming with the mail from the station an auto struck his baggy. He was thrown to the ground and suffered minor injuries which will necessitate his staying in for several days. A beautiful wedding was performed by Ward Chapel, A. M. Summer Sunday, November 29, when Miss Irine Bisch *M. Mr. P. L. Grey Sot of crystal City*. The bride was beautifully gowned in white geogette and carried a bouquet of yellow and white chrysanthemums. Miss Bernice Marshall, the bridesmaid, were blue shiffen and carried a bouquet of white roses. *Mr. Harry Bisch, brother of the bride*, was best man. The ceremony was performed by Rey, W. H. The church was beautifully decorated and the pastor served with the church choir, training to Harvest. *Thanksgiving services and dinners at the churches* *Rev W. H. Spurlock has gone for a short visit and will accompany Mrs. Spurlock home*. Marshall, and Mrs Cassius Grey made a trip to St. Louis*. *Mrs Wallace with of Desoto attended the widow

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: COLORED SORORITY MEETS IN A WHITE UNIVERSITY

regions.label: text
regions.text: The Associated Negro Press

regions.label: text
regions.text: PHILADELPHIA, Pa., Nov. 23- The third annual convention of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority will be held in the Engineering building of the University of Pennsylvania, December 27-29 inclusive. This is the first time that a Colored Sorority has held its convention within the walls of a white University. It is indicative both of the standing of Delta Sigma Theta, the Pennsylvania and the spirit of that school that this privilege was granted. To the Convention will come delegates from coast to coast. Delta Sigma Theta was, the first of our Greek letter societies to form a chapter on the Pacific coast. On Feb. established to the University of Calcutta 78th. 1621. Kappa Chapter was fortune of Jesse Cal. a delegate to the 1940s well as a member of the other fourteen chapters of the sorority will be present at the Convention. Philadelphia looks forward with great pleasure to the reception of the least ten delegates. Delta Womens from the leading colleges of the country.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: INDUSTRIAL COMMISSION ON HOUSING CONDITIONS

regions.label: text
regions.text: Special to The. Argus.

regions.label: text
regions.text: JEFFERSON CITY, Mo. Nov. 14—According to recent statistics received by Robert S. Cobb of the Missouri Negro Industrial Commission, there were 774 black inmages soilling sourabag, 714 black inmages nobilage, 780 deaths were caused by some form of Tuberculosis. Mortality statistics by principal cities is as follows:

regions.label: text
regions.text:
St. Louis, 1,359; Kansas City, 694;
Jefferson City, 24; Sedalia, 47.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Of the 3,674 deaths 2377 were in the larger cities. Poor housing facilities in the congested areas of the larger cities is responsible for $n$ large num-  
mium housing missions is working in harmony with the Urban League and other welfare agencies in Missouri in lower the death rate among Negroes in Missouri.
